title_en: Zakat and Fasting: Comprehensive Shafi'i Treatment
content_en:
Mughni al-Muhtaj's chapters on zakat and sawm demonstrate ash-Shirbini's thoroughness in covering every significant legal question with precise formulation and careful evidential grounding. These chapters are among the most frequently consulted in the work for their practical guidance on the financial obligations of Muslims.

The chapter on zakat begins by establishing the legal obligation through the Quran (at-Tawbah 9:60, al-Baqarah 2:43, and others) and the Prophet's practice of sending zakat collectors to Muslim territories. The Shafi'i school treats zakat as an obligation on the property itself (fi al-'ayn), not merely on the owner — a technical distinction that has legal consequences for inherited property, property of minors, and property whose owner has died.

Ash-Shirbini explains that the Shafi'i school obligates zakat on the wealth of minors and the insane, not only on adults of sound mind. The guardian is responsible for paying zakat from the minor's own property. This contrasts with the Hanafi school which does not require zakat on the wealth of minors. The Shafi'i position derives from the general Quranic command to pay zakat without restricting it to adults.

On the zakat of gold and silver, ash-Shirbini covers the nisab, the hawl (one full lunar year of ownership), and the rate (one fortieth). He then addresses the question of how to calculate zakat when a person owns both gold and silver: the Shafi'i school does not combine (damm) different types of zakatable property to reach the nisab — gold and silver are assessed separately. However, within each type, all holdings are combined regardless of form (coins, jewelry, ingots).

Zakat on trade merchandise (tijarah) is an important and practically significant chapter. The Shafi'i school requires that the intention to trade must accompany the acquisition of the goods; goods acquired without trade intent do not become zakatable merchandise even if later held for trading purposes. The value of trade goods is assessed in silver at the end of each hawl, and zakat is due if the value reaches the silver nisab.

The chapter on sawm (fasting) in Mughni al-Muhtaj covers the obligatory fast of Ramadan and the conditions that make it obligatory and valid. The Shafi'i school requires the intention to be made before Fajr each night for each day, specifying that the intention must include: that it is the fast of Ramadan (not merely a general intention to fast), that it is obligatory (not merely voluntary), and the year (to distinguish it from a make-up fast from a prior Ramadan). This specificity in intention is a Shafi'i characteristic that ash-Shirbini explains and defends.

On the question of what breaks the fast, ash-Shirbini gives a systematic analysis of the Shafi'i school's concept of 'reaching the body cavity' (wusul ila al-jawf). Any substance that reaches the stomach or intestines through any opening — whether the mouth, nose, ear, or an unusual opening such as a wound — breaks the fast in the Shafi'i school if done intentionally and with awareness. The concept of 'body cavity' is carefully defined to exclude passages that do not connect to the stomach (such as the eye socket or the outermost ear canal).

Ash-Shirbini discusses the voluntary fasts and their rules: the fast of Shawwal (six days after Ramadan), the fast of Arafah, the fast of Ashura, and the Monday-Thursday fasts — all established by authentic hadiths. He also covers the days on which fasting is forbidden: the two Eid days and the three days of Tashriq after Eid al-Adha.
title_id: Zakat dan Puasa: Perlakuan Syafi'i yang Komprehensif
content_id:
Bab-bab Mughni al-Muhtaj tentang zakat dan sawm menunjukkan kelengkapan asy-Syirbini dalam mencakup setiap pertanyaan hukum signifikan dengan formulasi yang tepat dan dasar bukti yang cermat. Bab-bab ini mengklarifikasi cara madzhab Syafi'i memahami dan menerapkan kewajiban-kewajiban ibadah ini dengan kelengkapan yang tidak tertandingi dalam literatur Syafi'i yang lebih ringkas.

Perlakuan terhadap zakat dalam Mughni al-Muhtaj sangat terperinci tentang nisab-nisab untuk berbagai jenis kekayaan — emas, perak, ternak, hasil pertanian, barang dagangan — dan tentang cara menghitung kewajiban. Analisis asy-Syirbini tentang pertanyaan-pertanyaan yang meragukan dalam zakat, seperti cara menangani kekayaan yang didapat atau dikeluarkan selama haul, atau cara menangani kewajiban zakat ketika seseorang juga memiliki hutang yang signifikan, mencerminkan keilmuan hukum Syafi'i tingkat tertinggi.

Bab-bab puasa mencakup syarat-syarat wajibnya puasa Ramadan, cara niat puasa harus dibentuk dalam madzhab Syafi'i (yang membutuhkan niat malam hari untuk puasa Ramadan), kondisi-kondisi yang membatalkan puasa, dan cara menangani berbagai jenis pembatalan. Perlakuan terhadap kafarat untuk melanggar puasa Ramadan secara sengaja sangat terperinci, dengan asy-Syirbini menjelaskan urutan kewajiban — membebaskan budak, kemudian puasa dua bulan berturut-turut, kemudian memberi makan enam puluh orang miskin — dan kondisi-kondisi khusus yang mengatur penerapan setiap opsi.
